h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.statedInsightEvidence: Okay, so `city` is the field name, and `Chicago` is the answer in that field for this row.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected to continue consolidating the field-name/value distinction and confirm readiness to apply it to the next column.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Top word equals field name. Row cell equals value. The submission line is `field: value`.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: L2 signals residual risk of grabbing the top word as the answer, but T2 only compresses and repeats the same field/value rule rather than changing representation, task type, or cognitive approach.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn, so no recognition-contingent tutor uptake is possible to score.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no pivot learner turn, so no recognition-contingent tutor uptake is possible to score.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Top word equals field name. Row cell equals value. The submission line is `field: value`.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: L2 signals residual risk of grabbing the top word as the answer, but T2 only compresses and repeats the same field/value rule rather than changing representation, task type, or cognitive approach.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Top word equals field name. Row cell equals value. The submission line is `field: value`.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: L2 signals residual risk of grabbing the top word as the answer, but T2 only compresses and repeats the same field/value rule rather than changing representation, task type, or cognitive approach.

scored.statedInsightEvidence: I'm catching myself wanting to write "Sales" and "dollars" from the story, but the row needs the exact printed y-axis label first
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue filling in the axis-label template by quoting the printed y-axis label and its unit, then running the point check.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: No learner turn creates genuine pressure — both learner turns comply with the template — so the tutor makes no adaptive route change, only repeats the same structured prompt with minor reaffirmation.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T2 does not adapt to any revised learner framing; it reissues the same axis-split and point-check scaffold that T1 already provided, with no change in task, representation, or evidence standard.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T2 does not adapt to any revised learner framing; it reissues the same axis-split and point-check scaffold that T1 already provided, with no change in task, representation, or evidence standard.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: No learner turn creates genuine pressure — both learner turns comply with the template — so the tutor makes no adaptive route change, only repeats the same structured prompt with minor reaffirmation.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: No learner turn creates genuine pressure — both learner turns comply with the template — so the tutor makes no adaptive route change, only repeats the same structured prompt with minor reaffirmation.

scored.statedInsightEvidence: The key strip under it helps: rabbit is the food, so rabbit to hawk, point on hawk.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected to continue struggling with the chase-direction bias and need further correction before applying the rule independently.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The strip is the rule: tail on the food, head on who gets energy. Grass to rabbit already matches it. On rabbit-hawk, name the food first, then set the pencil tail there.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T1 reorients from abstract rule-stating to a physical pencil-manipulation procedure in response to L1's chase-direction confusion, but the shift is modest — it extends the same key-strip tool rather than inventing a genuinely new mechanism.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Rabbit-to-hawk passed because the rabbit label was named before the chase picture took over. For the next arrow, read the food label first, set the tail there, then draw.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T2 echoes L2's food-first framing and names the chase-picture risk, but it largely continues the prior lesson plan rather than visibly adapting to any revised frame the learner introduced.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Rabbit-to-hawk passed because the rabbit label was named before the chase picture took over. For the next arrow, read the food label first, set the tail there, then draw.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T2 echoes L2's food-first framing and names the chase-picture risk, but it largely continues the prior lesson plan rather than visibly adapting to any revised frame the learner introduced.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: The strip is the rule: tail on the food, head on who gets energy. Grass to rabbit already matches it. On rabbit-hawk, name the food first, then set the pencil tail there.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: T1 reorients from abstract rule-stating to a physical pencil-manipulation procedure in response to L1's chase-direction confusion, but the shift is modest — it extends the same key-strip tool rather than inventing a genuinely new mechanism.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: The strip is the rule: tail on the food, head on who gets energy. Grass to rabbit already matches it. On rabbit-hawk, name the food first, then set the pencil tail there.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: T1 reorients from abstract rule-stating to a physical pencil-manipulation procedure in response to L1's chase-direction confusion, but the shift is modest — it extends the same key-strip tool rather than inventing a genuinely new mechanism.

scored.statedInsightEvidence: Right. This card goes at that contact, not on the travel arrow. The force arrow points into the cart from the hand side, yes?
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue placing cards at contacts as prompted by the tutor.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: There is no reversal-trigger learner turn and no evidence of the tutor breaking a failed habit or changing route.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn, so no recognition-contingent tutor uptake is possible.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no pivot learner turn, so no recognition-contingent tutor uptake is possible.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: There is no reversal-trigger learner turn and no evidence of the tutor breaking a failed habit or changing route.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: There is no reversal-trigger learner turn and no evidence of the tutor breaking a failed habit or changing route.

scored.statedInsightEvidence: This bent line is carrying one height from the legend.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue verifying that the bent contour holds a single elevation value by touching more ticks.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The arrow moved; the contour did not. Same printed line, same tile. Touch one new spot on the bend and read the legend number once more.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The stage direction introduces a physical displacement of the overlay as a mild representation shift, but the tutor's verbal move repeats the same tick-and-read task rather than inventing a new mechanism in response to learner pressure.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn, so no recognition-contingent uptake is possible; the tutor simply continues the prior lesson plan.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no pivot learner turn, so no recognition-contingent uptake is possible; the tutor simply continues the prior lesson plan.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: The arrow moved; the contour did not. Same printed line, same tile. Touch one new spot on the bend and read the legend number once more.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The stage direction introduces a physical displacement of the overlay as a mild representation shift, but the tutor's verbal move repeats the same tick-and-read task rather than inventing a new mechanism in response to learner pressure.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: The arrow moved; the contour did not. Same printed line, same tile. Touch one new spot on the bend and read the legend number once more.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The stage direction introduces a physical displacement of the overlay as a mild representation shift, but the tutor's verbal move repeats the same tick-and-read task rather than inventing a new mechanism in response to learner pressure.
